question: The synonym for water gas when used in the production of methanol is:
solution:
Water gas is produced by the reaction of hot coke with blast of steam.

C+H2O→CO+H2 (Water gas or Syn gas);

Water gas is also called syn gas because it is used for synthesis of methanol.

subject: Chemistry
q: An indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ is used for studying the effect of variation in concentration of iodide on the rate of reaction of iodide ion with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at room temp. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ forms blue colored complex with compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ present in the solution. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ and compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ respectively are
question: An indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ is used for studying the effect of variation in concentration of iodide on the rate of reaction of iodide ion with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at room temp. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ forms blue colored complex with compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ present in the solution. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ and compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ respectively are
solution:
The reaction between iodide ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) occurs in the acidic medium and can be represented in the following manner:

2I- (aq) + H2O2 (l) + 2H+ (aq) → I2 (g) + 2H2O (l)

In this reaction, hydrogen peroxide oxidises iodide ions (I-) to molecular iodine. If a calculated amount of sodium thiosulphate is added in the presence of starch solution as an indicator to the above reaction mixture, the liberated iodine reacts with thiosulphate ions as fast as it is formed and is reduced back to iodide ions till all the thiosulphate ions are oxidised to tetrathionate ions.

I2 (g) + 2S2O32- (aq) → S4O62- (aq) + 2I- (aq)

After the complete consumption of thiosulphate ions, the concentration of iodine liberated in the reaction of hydrogen peroxide with iodide ions increases rapidly to a point where iodine forms intense blue complex with starch.

B. Heavy water is prepared by exhaustive electrolysis of water.

C. Heavy water has higher boiling point than ordinary water.

D. Viscosity of H2O is greater than D2O.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
Heavy water is used in exchange reactions for study of reaction mechanisms Heavy water is prepared by exhaustive electrolysis of water.

B.P. of D2O=374.4 K

B.P. of H2O=373 K

Viscosity of H2O=0.89 centipoise

Viscosity of D2O=1.107 centipoise

subject: Chemistry
q:
The strength of 11.2 volume solution of H2O2 is

[Given that, the molar mass of H=1 g mol-1 and O=16 g mol-1]
question:
The strength of 11.2 volume solution of H2O2 is

[Given that, the molar mass of H=1 g mol-1 and O=16 g mol-1]
solution:
Molarity of H2O=Volume strength11.2

M=11.211.2=1

∴ 1 molar means 1000 mL solution contains 1 mol H2O2=1×34 g

Since, the strength is represented as weight of the solute in 1 L of the solution. The strength of the solution will be 34 g L-1.

Note: The question should've mentioned that we need to find the mass by volume percentage.

Percentage w/v =341000×100=3.4%

subject: Chemistry
q: Boiling of hard water is helpful in removing the temporary hardness by converting calcium hydrogen carbonate and magnesium hydrogen carbonate to
question: Boiling of hard water is helpful in removing the temporary hardness by converting calcium hydrogen carbonate and magnesium hydrogen carbonate to
solution:
During boiling MgHCO32 is converted to insoluble MgOH2

MgHCO32→BoilingMgOH2↓+2CO2

CaHCO32→boilingCaCO3↓+H2O+CO2

CaHCO32 changes to insoluble CaCO3

Mg(OH)2​ is less soluble than MgCO3. Hence, the products formed are CaCO3 and Mg(OH)2.​

(b) aqueous sodium hydroxide.

question:

(a) dilute hydrochloric acid and
(b) aqueous sodium hydroxide.

solution:

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Physical properties of isotopes of hydrogen are different.

Reason R : Mass difference between isotopes of hydrogen is very large.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Physical properties of isotopes of hydrogen are different.

Reason R : Mass difference between isotopes of hydrogen is very large.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
The isotopes of hydrogen are protium, deuterium, and tritium, which have different physical properties. For example, deuterium has a higher boiling point and a lower vapor pressure than protium, while tritium is radioactive and has a much shorter half-life than the other two isotopes.

The mass difference between the isotopes of hydrogen is indeed significant, with protium having a mass of 1 atomic mass unit (amu), deuterium having a mass of 2 amu, and tritium having a mass of 3 amu.

So Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Chemistry
q: In comparison to the zeolite process for the removal of permanent hardness, the synthetic resin method is
question: In comparison to the zeolite process for the removal of permanent hardness, the synthetic resin method is
options: less efficient as it exchanges only anions.
more efficient as it can exchange both cations as well as anions.
less efficient as the resins cannot be regenerated.
more efficient as it can exchange only cations.
solution:
Zeolite removes only cations from hard water, as given below by reaction:

2 NaZ + M+2(aq)→MZ2 + 2 Na+ Here M-Ca, Mg

While, synthetic resin removes both cations and anions, as given by following reactions:

For cations-

2 RNa(s) +M+2(aq)→ R2M (s)+ 2Na+(aq)

For anions-

RNH3+OH-(s) + X-(aq) →RNH3+X- (s)+ OH-(aq)

So, synthetic resin method is more efficient as it can exchange both cations as well anions.

a Saline hydrides produce H2 gas when reacted with H2O.

b Reaction of LiAlH4 with BF3 leads to B2H6 .

c PH3 and CH4 are electron - rich and electron - precise hydrides, respectively.

d HF and CH4 are called as molecular hydrides.
question:

options: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>  and  <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>  only.

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>  and  <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>  only.

<math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
(1) Saline hydrides are ionic hydrides such as LiH, NaH. When reacting with water to form a Base and hydrogen gas.

MHs+H2Oaq ⟶MOHaq+H2

(2) BF3 when reacts with LiAlH4 produces B2H6

(3)  

(4) Molecular hydrides are also called covalent hydrides. HF and CH4 are covalent or molecular hydrides.

Hence, all statements are correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements about water is FALSE?
question: Which of the following statements about water is FALSE?
options: There is extensive intramolecular hydrogen bonding in the condensed phase.
Ice formed by heavy water sinks in normal water.
Water is oxidized to oxygen during photosynthesis
Water can act both as an acid and as a base
solution:
(i) Ice formed by heavy water sinks in normal water due to higher density of D2O than normal water.

(ii) 6 CO 2 + 6 H 2 O → chlorophyll hv C 6 H 12 O 6 +6O 2

(iii) Water can show amphiprotic nature and hence water can act both as an acid a base.

(iv) There is extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding in the condensed phase instead of intramolecular H - bonding.

It reacts with  <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math>  to produce  <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math>  and  <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> .

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> acts as a reducing agent in
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> acts as a reducing agent in
solution:
Here, the H2O2 act as the reducing agent, as the Cl has +1 oxidation state of in NaOCl and after reaction with the H2O2, in the presence of the acid, it reduces the Cl in NaOCl to Cl- having -1 oxidation state.

So, as Cl oxidation state decreases from the +1 to -1 oxidation state, hence the H2O2 is acting as the reducing agent.

subject: Chemistry
q:
In which of the following reactions H2O2 acts as a reducing agent ?

(a)   H 2 O 2 + 2 H + + 2 e - → 2 H 2 O

(b)   H 2 O 2 - 2 e - → O 2 + 2 H +

(c)   H 2 O 2 + 2 e - → 2 OH -

(d)   H 2 O 2 + 2 OH - - 2 e - → O 2 + 2 H 2 O
question:
In which of the following reactions H2O2 acts as a reducing agent ?

(a)   H 2 O 2 + 2 H + + 2 e - → 2 H 2 O

(b)   H 2 O 2 - 2 e - → O 2 + 2 H +

(c)   H 2 O 2 + 2 e - → 2 OH -

(d)   H 2 O 2 + 2 OH - - 2 e - → O 2 + 2 H 2 O
solution:
Reducing agent undergoes oxidation and in oxidation half reaction there will be loss of electrons.


(b)   H 2 O 2 → O 2 + 2 H + + 2 e - (loss)

(d)   H 2 O 2 + OH - → 2 H 2 O + O 2 + 2 e - (loss)

Statement I : Stannane is an example of a molecular hydride.

options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
Both Statement I and Statement II are false.
Statement I is true but Statement II is false.
Statement I is false but Statement II is true.
solution:
SnH4 is a non-planar molecular hydride. Molecular hydrides are again classified in to different types.SnH4 is electron precise type of molecular hydride.

In SnH4, the central atom tin is sp3 hybridized, Hence, the molecule has tetrahedral shape.It is not planar

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following can reduce decomposition of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on exposure to light
question: Which of the following can reduce decomposition of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on exposure to light
solution: H2O2 decomposes on exposure to sunlight or on heating. It also decomposes on standing but at a slow rate. These reactions occur explosively when H2O2 is in contact with dust. Urea acts as a negative catalyst to this reaction and slow down the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.

q: The products obtained during treatment of hard water using Clark's method are
question: The products obtained during treatment of hard water using Clark's method are
solution:
In this method calculated amount of lime is added to hard water. It precipitates out calcium carbonate and magnesium hydroxide which can be filtered off In Clark's method lime water is used.

It is because of low solubility product of Mg(OH)2 as compared to that of MgCO3.

CaHCO32+2CaOH2→2CaCO3+2H2O

MgHCO32+2CaOH2→2CaCO3+MgOH2+2H2O

q:
Given below are two reactions, involved in the commercial production of dihydrogen H2. The two reactions are carried out at temperature “T1” and “T2”, respectively

Cs+H2Og⟶T1COg+H2g

COg+H2OgT2CatalystCO2g+H2g

The temperatures T1 and T2 are correctly related as
question:

Cs+H2Og⟶T1COg+H2g

COg+H2OgT2CatalystCO2g+H2g

The temperatures T1 and T2 are correctly related as
solution:
Cs+H2Og⟶1270 K T1COg+H2g is known as coal gasification reaction. The mixture produced in this reaction is called water gas.

COg+H2Og673 K T2Catalyst→CO2g+H2g is known as water gas shift reaction. Using water gas shift reaction we can increase the concentration of hydrogen gas.

Hence, T1>T2

q: The reaction of zinc with excess of aqueous alkali, evolves hydrogen gas and gives
question: The reaction of zinc with excess of aqueous alkali, evolves hydrogen gas and gives
solution:
Zn+2NaOH→Na2ZnO2+H2↑

In the above reaction, Zinc reacts with Sodium Hydroxide, gives Sodium Zincate with evolving of hydrogen. Here Zn does not react as an acid in the reaction above since the reaction is not an acid-base one, but rather oxidation-reduction

ZnO22- is anhydrous form of ZnOH42-

subject: Chemistry
q: Water does not produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> on reacting with :
question: Water does not produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> on reacting with :
solution:
Carbon monoxide does not react with water to form an acid.

The carbon monoxide molecule is one that won't react with water at all. As the acidic oxide has a higher oxidation state, it reacts with water to produce acid, and reacts with base to produce salt. 

CO2+H2O→H2CO3

C+H2Osteam→CO+H2

CH4+H2O→CO+3H2C3H8+H2O→3CO+H2 both reactions are carried out at 1270 K temp. with Ni catalyst

q: On reaction with stronger oxidizing agent like <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KIO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math>, hydrogen peroxide oxidizes with the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The oxidation number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KIO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> changes to
question: On reaction with stronger oxidizing agent like <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KIO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math>, hydrogen peroxide oxidizes with the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The oxidation number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>KIO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> changes to
solution:
Hydrogen peroxide acts as an oxidising as well as reducing agent in both acidic and alkaline media.

But its reducing action is more effective in alkaline medium

KIO4+H2O2→KIO3+O2+H2O

The oxidation number of iodine in IO3- is +5

The geometry of iodine containing compound changes from tetrahedral to pyramidal in the above reaction

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements, one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Isotopes of hydrogen have almost same chemical properties, but difference in their rates of reactions.

Reason R: Isotopes of hydrogen have different enthalpy of bond dissociation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements, one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Isotopes of hydrogen have almost same chemical properties, but difference in their rates of reactions.

Reason R: Isotopes of hydrogen have different enthalpy of bond dissociation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
A is not correct but R is correct
A is correct but R is not correct
solution: While isotopes of an element have the same electronic configuration, their chemical properties can differ slightly due to differences in their atomic mass. Isotopes of an element have the same number of protons but varying numbers of neutrons, which affects the overall mass of the atom. The only difference is in their rates of reactions, mainly due to their different enthalpy of bond dissociation.

question: In which of the following reactions, hydrogen peroxide acts as an oxidizing agent?
solution:
The reaction in which H2O2 is getting reduced, it will behave as oxidising agent.

2e-+2H++H2O2⟶2H2O   (reduction)

S-2+4H2O⟶SO4-2+8H++8e-(oxidation)

Equation. 1×4+Equation.2 

Final reaction PbS+4H2O2⟶PbSO4+4H2O

subject: Chemistry
q: Which hydride among the following is less stable?
question: Which hydride among the following is less stable?
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BeH</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is indeed considered to be less stable compared to other hydrides among the given due to its significant covalent character and electron deficiency. Beryllium, being a small and highly charged cation, attracts the electrons in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Be</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> bonds more strongly, resulting in a greater degree of electron sharing between beryllium and hydrogen. This covalent character is in contrast to other alkaline earth metal hydrides, which tend to have more ionic character.
